sections.content: You do not need to master Latin grammar to pray in Latin. But understanding noun endings helps you recognize what role a word plays in a prayer — is it the subject? The object? Is something being offered TO someone? This course covers the two most common noun patterns.

sections.title: Why Endings Matter
sections.content:
In English, word order tells you who does what: 'God loves man' vs 'Man loves God.' In Latin, ENDINGS tell you who does what. Word order is flexible.

'Deus hóminem amat' = God loves man
'Hóminem Deus amat' = God loves man (same meaning, different order)

The ending '-em' on 'hominem' tells you it is the object. The ending '-us' on 'Deus' tells you it is the subject. You will see these patterns in every prayer.

sections.title: First Declension (-a words)
sections.content:
Most first declension nouns are feminine. They are the '-a' words you hear constantly in prayers.

Singular:
• María (Mary) — subject
• Maríam — object ('Hail Mary' / addressing Mary)
• Maríæ — 'of Mary' or 'to Mary'

Common first declension words in prayers:
• grátia (grace) → grátiam, grátiæ
• glória (glory) → glóriam, glóriæ
• vita (life) → vitam, vitæ
• terra (earth) → terram, terræ
• Ecclésia (Church) → Ecclésiam, Ecclésiæ
• misericórdia (mercy) → misericórdiam

When you see '-am,' something is being done TO that thing.
When you see '-æ,' it means 'of' or 'to' that thing.

sections.title: Second Declension (-us words)
sections.content:
Most second declension nouns are masculine. They are the '-us' words.

Singular:
• Dóminus (Lord) — subject
• Dóminum — object
• Dómini — 'of the Lord'
• Dómino — 'to/for the Lord'

Common second declension words:
• Deus (God) → Deum, Dei, Deo
• Fílius (Son) → Fílium, Fílii, Fílio
• Christus → Christum, Christi, Christo
• peccátum (sin) → peccáta (plural!)
• cælum (heaven) → cæli, cælo
• verbum (word) → verbi, verbo

Note: '-um' words (like peccátum, cælum, verbum) are neuter — they follow the same pattern but their plural ends in '-a': peccáta (sins), sǽcula (ages).

items.note: María = subject (1st decl), Dei = 'of God' (2nd decl genitive)
débita = neuter plural object (2nd decl), nostra = 'our'
vitam = object (1st decl -am), ætérnam = adjective matching

sections.content: First declension: -a words (mostly feminine). -am = object, -æ = of/to. Second declension: -us words (masculine), -um words (neuter). -um = object, -i = of, -o = to/for. You do not need to memorize charts — just recognize these endings when you see them in prayers, and the meaning becomes clearer.
